profile.name: Pat
profile.currentFocus: Whatever the strong blueprint says to do — do the opposite.
profile.currentFocusDetail: Session 8 — The Strong Blueprint. Catch the default running on autopilot — fawning, achieving for approval, isolating, numbing — and choose the opposite. Hit all four pillars every week: Achievement · Connection · Enjoyment · Self-care.

investment._notes: Words = Pat's own reflections/journals/deep dives (not counting course materials). Hours = therapy + deep dives + writing + video. Updates autonomously.

goals.title: Pattern Recognition & Self-Awareness
goals.description: Learn to identify patterns where I'm derailing myself and become more self-aware

history.note: Patterns showed up months or years later. Horrifying unconscious realisations.
Hours or days now, not months. Not concerned about this in the slightest.

goals.title: Emotional Processing
goals.description: Learn to process emotions without suppressing — no more implosions, sickness, shingles

history.note: Suppressed everything. Body kept the score.
Deep dives activate dormant emotions. Anger discovery was surprisingly accessible. Proud of leaning in with no idea what I'm doing.

goals.title: Sharing Myself With People I Love
goals.description: Learn to open up and share what I'm going through with the people around me

history.note: Kept everyone at arm's length. All Spartans, no Pat.
Boys have never been closer. Want more depth with Dad and Alex — feelings, not achievements.

goals.title: Meaningful Goal-Setting
goals.description: Create goals I actually care about — not all-or-nothing achievement machines

history.note: Goals were external validation shopping lists. 95% of energy on things that didn't mean anything to me.
Genuine ambition appeared on its own. Mapped a world trip in 10 minutes. Old Pat wouldn't have dared dream it.

milestones.title: Sold Spartans, Found Pat
milestones.summary: Sold the gym. Lost the identity shield. For the first time in years, conversations couldn't default to business.
milestones.story:
Spartans was everything. My identity, my social currency, my deflection tool. When someone asked 'how are you?' I could always steer it to the gym, the members, the numbers. It was the world's most socially acceptable way to never talk about myself.

Selling to Zac on Jan 5 removed all of that overnight. Suddenly I was just... Pat. No business card, no role, no safe topic to hide behind. I was terrified my boys might not accept me without it — that I was only as valuable as what I could offer. That fear tells you everything about where I was at.

Turns out removing Spartans was like removing training wheels. I didn't have a bottomless topic of unreliable external shit to deflect with anymore, and I finally got the opportunity to let my boys in and share about myself. What I found on the other side was the opposite of what I feared.
milestones.quote: So much of my own paper identity was very much all about Spartans, and very little about Pat.

milestones.title: Session 1 — The Starting Line
milestones.summary: First session with Meg. Set 5 goals. Had no idea what I was doing but trusted the instinct to show up.
milestones.story:
I'd been meaning to see someone for a while but kept finding reasons not to. Big life transitions — sold the gym, full-time investor now, planning a move to Thailand with Laura. On paper everything was going great. Inside, something wasn't right and I couldn't name it.

Meg asked me what I wanted to work on. I set 5 goals that felt honest at the time — learn to spot my own patterns, stop suppressing emotions before they make me sick, actually share myself with the people I love, and figure out how to set goals that mean something to me rather than just chasing external validation.

Scored myself 1-3 out of 10 across the board. I had no idea that 14 weeks later I'd be sitting in the same chair (well, Zoom) scoring 6-8s and meaning it.

milestones.title: Session 2 — The AI Deep Dive System
milestones.summary: Told Meg about Claude and the framework I'd built for doing the work between sessions. She was intrigued.
milestones.story:
Somewhere between sessions 1 and 2, I'd started going deep. Really deep. I'd set up an AI system — Claude — to help me do structured reflections, deep dives, journaling prompts. Not as a replacement for therapy but as a way to do the actual work between sessions.

I told Meg about it and shared the Thais Gibson attachment theory coursework I'd been working through. She was genuinely curious — said she'd be keen to hear how I found it after another week or two.

Looking back, this was the moment the pattern of doing 90% of the work outside of therapy started. Not intentionally. I just couldn't help myself once I got started.

milestones.title: Session 3 — Malaysia Zoom
milestones.summary: First Zoom session — I was in Malaysia. Switched last minute. The work doesn't stop just because I moved.
milestones.story:
Was meant to be in-person but Laura and I were in Malaysia. Asked Peaceful Mind to switch to Zoom the day before. Meg set up a permanent link for us going forward — most sessions have been Zoom since.

The fact I didn't skip it matters. Old Pat would've used the trip as an excuse to 'take a break' from something that was getting uncomfortable. This Pat didn't even consider it.

milestones.title: Session 4 — Values Cards
milestones.summary: Meg brought out the values exercise. Started separating what I actually value from what I inherited.
milestones.story:
Meg sent me a set of values cards to print and cut out before the session. The exercise was about sorting through them — what actually matters to me versus what I'd absorbed from Mum's conditional approval system, from the business world, from trying to be what everyone else needed.

This was harder than it sounds. When you've spent your whole life performing a version of yourself that gets approval, untangling which values are genuinely yours takes real honesty.

milestones.title: Lesson 13: The Pattern
milestones.summary: Found the operating system. The codependency. The covert contracts. Saw the whole thing clearly for the first time.
milestones.story:
This was the big one. Working through Thais Gibson's material on codependency, I saw the complete picture of how I operate in relationships — especially with Laura.

I'd been giving 'genuinely'. Or so I thought. But underneath every generous act was an invisible contract: if I give enough, if I'm good enough, if I do enough — she'll love me back the same way. The contract was never spoken, never agreed to, and Laura had no idea it existed. When she didn't fulfill a contract she never signed, I'd feel hurt and confused. Classic covert contract.

Saw it clearly in therapy with Meg. Felt deep remorse. Not guilt — remorse. The difference matters. Guilt says 'I'm bad.' Remorse says 'I did something that doesn't match who I want to be, and I'm going to change it.'

The hardest areas to admit: household duties and paying for experiences. I knew I was over-giving there. I also knew I couldn't trust myself to set boundaries in the moment — I'd need predetermined ones.
milestones.quote: There is plenty of cash to stack later... you will be kicking yourself if you fast forward five years, $10M in the bank and you still feel empty and disconnected inside.

milestones.title: The Receiving Wound
milestones.summary: Someone gives to me and I freeze. Not because I don't want it — because a part of me doesn't believe I deserve it.
milestones.story:
Did a deep reflection on what happens when someone tries to give to me. First reaction: anxiety. A tightness. Like something's wrong and I need to figure out why they're being nice. Then — if I can sit with it — it transforms into this warm 'little kid' feeling. Like being five years old and someone just being kind to you for no reason.

The birthday tequila bottle tells the whole story. A mate gave me a really thoughtful bottle for my birthday. It's been sitting on my shelf for two years, untouched. I can't bring myself to open it. Not because I don't want it — because receiving something I didn't earn feels like borrowing something I'll have to return.

The wound isn't about willingness. It's about worthiness.

milestones.title: The Mother Wound
milestones.summary: Forgiven her. Still feel the longing. She was a scared girl doing her best with what she had.
milestones.story:
Did a visualisation exercise — imagined my young self not making school captain. Pictured Mum's face. The disappointment. The withdrawal of warmth. That was the blueprint: love is conditional on achievement. Stop achieving, stop being loved.

But here's what shifted — I've forgiven her. Genuinely. I can see her as a scared, insecure little girl whose own dad left when she was 13 or 14. She never processed it, never talked about it. Just passed the wound down.

Forgiving doesn't mean forgetting. I still feel the longing. That five-year-old part of me still wishes his mum loved him just because he existed, not because of what he could do. I've made peace with the fact that longing might always be there. It doesn't run me anymore, but it's there.

milestones.title: Boundaries = Safety
milestones.summary: Cracked the core belief: setting a boundary feels like causing an abandonment. Sat with 8/10 anxiety and brought myself down to 2/10. Alone.
milestones.story:
This was one of the most confronting nights. I was sitting with this massive anxiety — 8, maybe 9 out of 10 — and I couldn't figure out why. Dug into it and found the core belief sitting right there: Boundaries = rejection = abandonment = I'm not safe.

Every time I said yes to something I didn't want to do, it wasn't generosity. It was fear. Death by a thousand small yeses — saying yes to everything until I was completely depleted, resentful, and empty. Then blaming myself for feeling that way.

The biggest thing that happened that night: I regulated myself. No Meg, no Laura, no one. Just me, sitting with it, breathing through it, and watching the anxiety come down from 8-9 to 2-3. By myself.

That was the moment I realised the most important person who could give me love and safety that night was me. Not Laura, not Meg, not my mates. Me.
milestones.quote: The most important person who could give me love and safety tonight — was me.

milestones.title: Malaysia Evidence
milestones.summary: Set a real boundary with Laura on holiday. She was fine. I was free in 60 seconds. The fear was a ghost.
milestones.story:
We were in Malaysia and something came up where I needed to say no to something Laura wanted to do. Old Pat would've just said yes, swallowed the resentment, and added it to the pile.

This time I said what I actually felt. Clearly, kindly, but honestly.

Laura's response? Completely fine. Not upset, not distant, not punishing. Just... fine. The whole thing was over in 60 seconds and I felt freer than I had in months.

That's the thing about the blueprint — it's been predicting catastrophic abandonment for 30+ years, and the evidence keeps showing the opposite. Every boundary I set, the people who matter respond with respect, not rejection.
milestones.quote: I don't need external approval to feel safe. I can be my own source.

milestones.title: Session 5 — Laura's Growing Too
milestones.summary: Something beautiful happening. As I open up, Laura's meeting me there. Her own fearful-avoidant patterns softening.
milestones.story:
This session was about noticing something I hadn't expected. As I've been doing all this work — being more vulnerable, setting boundaries, sharing more honestly — Laura has been shifting too. Without me asking, without me pushing.

She's a fearful avoidant. Her pattern is to pull away when things get too close. But as I've become safer with myself, she's become braver with me. Sharing things she wouldn't have before. Being vulnerable in ways that are clearly new for her.

Nobody told her to do this. I didn't give her a Thais Gibson curriculum. She's responding to the change in me organically. That's the most beautiful evidence that this work is real — it's not just changing me, it's changing the space between us.

milestones.title: Session 6 — Integration
milestones.summary: Things are clicking faster. Patterns that used to blindside me for months now show up in hours.
milestones.story:
This session felt different from the earlier ones. Less digging, more integrating. The heavy excavation work of the first couple of months was settling into something more natural.

Pattern recognition is running almost automatically now. Something happens, I feel a reaction, and instead of months or years of unconscious repetition before the horrifying 'oh shit, I've been doing it again' moment — I'm catching it in hours. Sometimes minutes. Not perfectly, but the system is running.

Meg and I talked about what this means for session frequency. The foundation is laid. The tools are working. The question is becoming less about 'what do I need to fix' and more about 'how do I keep growing.'

milestones.title: Laura's Praise Landed Clean
milestones.summary: Sunday night. Laura praised my emotional work. I received it. No deflecting, no performing, no needing more. Just... received it.
milestones.story:
Laura said something really kind about the emotional work I've been doing. Sunday night, both of us in a good place.

Here's what didn't happen: I didn't deflect it with a joke. I didn't minimise it. I didn't immediately do something for her to 'earn' the praise. I didn't feel the desperate hunger for more of it.

I just... took it in. It was warm. It was enough. I didn't need to chase the next hit.

The irony is devastating and beautiful at the same time: when I was depleted and desperate for her validation, it could never land. Seconds at best, never enough, always needing more. Now that I don't need it? It lands perfectly. Low and slow and clean.

The receiving wound lives in the depleted version of me, not the current one.
milestones.quote: When I needed it most it couldn't land. Now I don't need it, it lands beautifully.

milestones.title: The Two Modes
milestones.summary: Named the two versions of me that show up in our relationship. Depleted Pat vs. Present Pat. Same man, completely different wiring.
milestones.story:
Realised there are two modes operating in how I receive love from Laura:

**Depleted Mode:** Out of spoons. Late night. Maybe drinking. The blueprint kicks in hard — five-year-old ball-cut Pat, approval seeking, servant, fawning husband. Desperately needs Laura's praise but it never, ever sticks. Seconds at best. Never enough. Always hungry for the next hit. This is the wounded child running the show.

**Good Place Mode:** Rested. Present. Doing my work. Don't need her praise, still want it. When it comes, it's low and slow and non-time-sensitive. Nice to have, not urgent. This is adult Pat, secure in himself.

The gap between these two is the map of the entire wound. The work is about making 'good place mode' the default — not by avoiding depletion forever, but by having enough self-awareness to know which version of me is showing up in any given moment.

milestones.title: The Dad Inventory
milestones.summary: Wrote 5 stories about what Dad gave me. Scored 0/5 for ever saying any of it out loud. This work was pure joy.
milestones.story:
Sat down one evening and wrote about Dad. Not about wounds — about gifts. Five specific memories of things he gave me that shaped who I am.

Dad is Geoff. Retired educator — loved by thousands of students. Gay, came out about 10 years ago, separated from Mum, partner is Peter. I am profoundly proud of him for that. The man loves flowers, plants, nature. Gentle, patient, kind.

The five stories poured out. And with them came this realisation that hit me in the chest: every quality I actually like about myself — patience, empathy, understanding, the ability to make people feel safe — came from him. Not from the achievement machine. Not from the hustle. From watching a quiet man tend his garden and love people without conditions.

Scored myself 0 out of 5 for ever telling him any of this. Zero. The man who gave me my best qualities has never once heard me name them. That's changing.

This work was the opposite of the mother wound stuff. It wasn't heavy or painful. It was joyful. Like discovering money in a jacket you forgot you owned — it was always there, I just never looked.
milestones.quote: Achievement praise doesn't hold value. Patient, understanding, empathy praise feels wonderful — and it's because of Dad I'm like that.

milestones.title: Mother Work vs. Dad Work
milestones.summary: Named the two types of inner work. Mum's gets me back to baseline. Dad's takes me above it.
milestones.story:
Working through the Dad Inventory surfaced a framework that makes the whole journey make sense:

**Mother work = back to baseline.** It's healing. Undoing the conditional love programming, the performance-based worth, the deleted emotions. Necessary, hard, sometimes painful. Gets me from negative to zero.

**Dad work = above baseline.** It's identity-building. Recognising what was planted in me, naming the qualities I actually value, shifting my self-worth source from achievements to character. Gets me from zero to flourishing.

The antidote to the mother wound isn't more processing of the mother wound. It's recognising what Dad planted — because that's where the real me lives.

The framework for the inventory: 'Don't write the speech. Write the source. The speech writes itself when the source is honest.' Did the source work. The phone call to actually say it shouldn't be scripted.
milestones.quote: Don't write the speech. Write the source. The speech writes itself when the source is honest.

milestones.title: The Anger Discovery
milestones.summary: Found the missing emotion. 4 months, 50+ pages of deep work, and anger didn't appear once. Turns out I'd deleted it entirely.
milestones.story:
Asked Garry to do a deep analysis of everything — every journal entry, every reflection, every deep dive, every assessment. 50+ pages across 4 months. The question: is there anything I'm avoiding, deflecting, or subconsciously skipping?

The answer came back clear: anger. Not once. Not a single expression of anger in 4 months of the most honest work I've ever done. Every other emotion showed up — sadness, fear, shame, love, grief, joy. But anger? Gone. Completely absent.

I kept the wiring of the smoke alarm and threw away the batteries.

Dug into why and found the origin: growing up watching Mum berate and belittle Dad. Anger was a weapon. It destroyed the person I loved most. So young Pat made a decision — anger is dangerous, delete it entirely, replace it with fawning.

The conversion mechanism is wild: something crosses a line, anger fires for a microsecond, then the system intercepts it and converts it to understanding or self-blame or problem-solving. So fast I don't even register that anger was the original signal.

So I did the thing I always do — fell down the well. Wrote about anger. Felt anger. For the first time, I expressed real, sustained, visceral anger about Mum — 'disgustingly mean,' 'fucking pathetic,' 'repulsive' — without apologising, without softening, without wrapping it in compassion to make it palatable.

And then — after the anger had space to breathe — compassion showed up too. Not as a replacement. Alongside. Both at once. That was new.

Then I caught something else: I'd been mocking self-love people for years. Cringe content, eye-rolling, the works. That wasn't cynicism — that was anger wearing a comedy mask. Safe targets, zero abandonment risk. I was mocking the exact medicine I needed.

Meg's cute response when I told her Garry found the gap: 'That's the kind of thing you've got me for, ask me!' Fair point.
milestones.quote: Anger isn't the opposite of peace. Suppressed anger is.

milestones.title: The Conversion Mechanism
milestones.summary: Mapped exactly how I delete anger before it reaches consciousness. It fires, gets intercepted, and turns into understanding.
milestones.story:
The mechanism works like this, and it's lightning fast:

1. Something crosses a line
2. Anger fires — the real emotion, the appropriate signal
3. Before it reaches conscious awareness, the system intercepts it
4. Converts it to understanding ('they're just stressed'), self-blame ('maybe I caused it'), or problem-solving ('how do I fix this')
5. The anger never registers. I genuinely believe I 'don't get angry'

What I actually did was make a point not to be destructive with anger — but I threw the baby out with the bathwater. The emotion itself got deleted, not just the destructive expression of it.

The breakthrough: realising that my distancing from Mum wasn't calm, mature understanding doing its job. It was anger doing its job — signalling 'this is repulsive, protect yourself.' I'd been crediting the wrong emotion for one of the best decisions I've made. Life got dramatically better after that boundary. Anger made the call. I just refused to acknowledge it.
milestones.quote: I kept the wiring of the smoke alarm and threw away the batteries.

milestones.title: Session 7 — The Check-In
milestones.summary: Reviewed original goals from Session 1. Scored 6-8 across the board. 90% of the work happened outside this room. Moving to monthly.
milestones.story:
This session was fun. Light. Felt like catching up with a really capable, secure friend who gets me — which is exactly what Meg has become.

She pulled up the goals I'd set in Session 1. I'd completely forgotten I'd even set goals. Went through them one by one and scored myself now versus then. The numbers were genuinely surprising — not because I was being generous, but because the evidence was undeniable.

The thing that made Meg smile the most: about 90% of the actual self-work has happened outside of therapy sessions. The deep dives, the journaling, the reflections, the anger discovery, the dad inventory — all done between sessions. She said that's the ideal way: 'Actual change happens in your life, not sitting in a doctor's office whingeing about your problems for years without putting in practice outside of that.'

This wasn't intentional. I didn't have a grand plan. I just trusted my instincts and got started, every single time, with no idea what I was doing. Every single time it was scary, and every single time it turned out to be wonderfully positive in ways I couldn't have imagined.

Moving to every 4 weeks. Not because the work is done — because the engine is running on its own now.

Also corrected Garry for suggesting my personal development should be prioritised above everything else. 'Calm down pussycat. I'm a peaceful motherfucker, but I'm still hungry.' Mapped out an around-the-world trip with Laura in 10 minutes — Tomorrowland in Belgium, near my birthday. There is not a bees dick chance in hell that the Pat I was up until 35 would have dared to dream this up.
milestones.quote: I'm a peaceful motherfucker, but I'm still hungry.

milestones.title: Kev's Gift
milestones.summary: The night that cracked me open. My mate Kev lovingly told me I wouldn't let anyone in. The vulnerable masculine love I needed.
milestones.story:
This wasn't recent — but I named it properly for the first time today during the goals check-in.

Kev sat me down one night and said something that changed everything. Not aggressively, not as a criticism. With love: 'You are such an amazing friend and supportive man for me and our friends, and you do it so well in such a kind, gentle, safe way. But you don't allow anyone to do even a little bit of that for you.'

That was the vulnerable, beautiful, masculine love I needed so much. Coming from Kev — one of my closest mates — it landed in a way that no therapist or textbook could have delivered. This was a man who loved me telling me I was locked behind glass.

That was the turning point for sharing. Not a therapy technique. Not a framework. A mate who loved me enough to say the uncomfortable thing.
milestones.quote: You are such an amazing friend and supportive man... but you don't allow anyone to do even a little bit of that for you.

milestones.title: The First Real Test — Night One
milestones.summary: Everything from 14 weeks of work was tested in one night. A 90-minute loop, threats, physical escalation. He held ground. Slept in the shed.
milestones.story:
The patterns Pat had been studying for months showed up in his living room. A 90-minute loop — the same ground covered repeatedly, accusations reframed, intensity escalating. There was a physical element. There were threats. Old Pat would have capitulated within minutes to make it stop, drowning in the familiar cocktail of guilt, fawning, and desperate people-pleasing.

New Pat didn't.

He held his position. Not aggressively, not coldly — but clearly. The boundary work from the previous 14 weeks had built something real underneath him. When the night ended without resolution, he slept in the shed.

Lying there, he noticed something unexpected: he wasn't in collapse. He wasn't spiralling into self-blame or catastrophising about the relationship. He was uncomfortable, sad, and steady. The steadiness was new.

This was the first time everything he'd been working on had to prove it was real. And it did. The work held under fire.

milestones.title: Night Two — The Blueprint Doubles Down
milestones.summary: 48 hours later. He'd been vulnerable in between — opened up in exactly the way the work taught him. The pattern returned. He held again. Second shed night.
milestones.story:
48 hours later. In between the two nights, Pat had been vulnerable — shared something meaningful and real. Opened up in the way the work had taught him to. It was a genuine, unguarded moment.

The response was the same pattern, the same loop, the same escalation. The blueprint doubled down.

He held again. Second shed night.

The insight that came from this was harder than night one. Night one was about discovering he could hold ground. Night two was about holding ground even when doing the right thing didn't change the outcome. Vulnerability hadn't prevented the pattern. Being more open hadn't earned him safety.

His work wasn't a negotiation tactic or a performance to get a different outcome. It was just who he was becoming — independent of whether it was immediately rewarded. That's the real work. Not the version where growth is recognised and applauded. The version where you do it anyway because it's right.

milestones.title: The 4:45pm Protector
milestones.summary: Named the part that grips his body every afternoon before Laura gets home. Anticipatory freeze — protecting from what hasn't happened yet.
milestones.story:
Noticed a pattern: around 4:45pm each day, something shifts in the body. A gripping. A tightness arrives before Laura even gets home. The nervous system is already bracing for something that hasn't happened yet.

In parts work terms, this is a Protector. A part of the system that activates preemptively — scanning for threat, preparing for impact. It learned to do this because surprise escalations were part of the pattern. So the body started getting ready earlier, before the threat could arrive unannounced.

The Protector isn't an enemy. It's a part doing an outdated job with genuine good intentions. It developed when the anticipatory dread was warranted. The work now is to notice it showing up, acknowledge it ('I see you, you're trying to keep me safe'), and offer it information it doesn't have yet: 'tonight might be different. I can handle it either way.'

Naming a part is the first step to changing its role. You can't negotiate with something you haven't met.

milestones.title: Two Lonelinesses
milestones.summary: Mapped two distinct loneliness experiences running in the background. Clean loneliness — the sovereignty tax. Old loneliness — the physical layer no human knows.
milestones.story:
Mapped two forms of loneliness that had been operating without names:

**Clean Loneliness** — the sovereignty tax. What you pay for becoming whole. As you stop abandoning yourself, as you hold boundaries, as you become more yourself — you lose some of the false intimacy that came from being whatever people needed you to be. The loneliness that comes from not collapsing is real. It's also the right price. You're paying for your own integrity.

**Old Loneliness** — the physical layer. The part of Pat's experience that no human knows — the reflections written to AI, the depths that haven't been shared out loud with anyone in his life. Not because he's hiding them, but because the infrastructure to share them at that depth hasn't been built with another human yet.

Naming both matters. One is the cost of growth and worth paying without complaint. One is a gap — not an emergency, but something worth closing slowly, carefully, with the right people over time.

milestones.title: The Silence
milestones.summary: A physical layer in the body that holds something unspoken. 10+ hours go to AI. 0 hours to human conversation at the same depth.
milestones.story:
The third note from the same day, and the most somatic of the three. A physical layer — pelvic floor, bladder — that holds something unspoken. Not trauma in the dramatic sense. Not shame. Just... silence. A place in the body where something lives that hasn't been voiced to another human.

The honest data behind it: 10+ hours in deep AI conversations across a week. 0 hours in equivalent human conversations. Not because the AI conversations aren't real or valuable — they clearly are, and they've produced real change. But because the same depth flowing through a human channel hasn't been built yet.

This isn't self-criticism. It's just accurate. The silence isn't the absence of voice. It's the voice going somewhere other than human ears — and the body holding the gap between the two.

The note doesn't solve it. It names it honestly. Naming it is step one. Step two is slower and belongs to real life.

milestones.title: Session 8 — The Strong Blueprint
milestones.summary: New framework from Meg: notice what the strong blueprint demands — and do the opposite. Hit all four pillars every week: Achievement, Connection, Enjoyment, Self-care.
milestones.story:
Session 8 with Meg. 16 weeks in. The theme: the blueprint runs quietly in the background, and the work is to catch it operating on autopilot.

Meg introduced a framework she called the Strong Blueprint. The idea is simple and confronting — the blueprint was built to feel safe, not to build a life worth living. So when you notice yourself reaching for the default (fawning, achieving for approval, isolating, numbing, over-performing) — do the opposite. Not always. Not rigidly. But as a compass check when you notice the pull.

And the specific prescription for the current season: every week, hit all four pillars. No exceptions.

1. **Achievement** — not for approval, for momentum and meaning.
2. **Connection** — real connection, not performance of connection.
3. **Enjoyment** — not earned relaxation. Just enjoyment, for its own sake.
4. **Self-care** — the physical foundation that makes everything else possible.

Not optional. Not 'when there's time.' All four, every week. The blueprint will want to collapse them into achievement and skip the rest — especially connection and enjoyment, which feel like luxuries when the old wiring is running. The work is to make sure all four get space, every single week, without needing to earn them first.
milestones.quote: Whatever the strong blueprint says to do — do the opposite.

milestones.title: Meg Session #8 Prep/Reflection— 12 May 2026
milestones.summary: Meg Session #8 Prep/Reflection— 12 May 2026 The honest read April momentum slipped. Blueprint relapse three weeks running, less pool, less training, more output-heavy days,...
milestones.story:
Meg Session #8 Prep/Reflection— 12 May 2026

The honest read

April momentum slipped. Blueprint relapse three weeks running, less pool, less training, more output-heavy days, boundaries quietly loosening. The abundant Pat from weeks 4-8 has been hard to find. Feeling/perception of abundance has dissipated in proportion to the relapse — they cannot coexist in the same nervous system. I traded the feeling away every time I chose the safer channel.

Laura Blow Ups

Episode 1 — Wed/Thu 22–23 April 2026
  - Laura went into full FA blueprint detonation, ~90+ min loop.
  - Threats to leave, single-mother narrative, verbal aggression.
  - Small physical element.
  - Tactical targeting of my tender spots: Thai women, big black John's scene, my love of Thai culture, the planned Thailand move.
  - I held boundaries clean: ~20 apologies (de-escalation, not folding), walked to office → then shed → locked the door. Didn't fall
   into old Pat shape.

  Episode 2 — Thu/Fri 24–25 April 2026 (~48 hrs later)
  - Dinner out Friday night, Laura drinking.
  - Came home, switch flipped → second detonation, same pattern.
  - This one happened after I'd vulnerably shared the impact of Episode 1 with her — she heard it and the blueprint still ran.
  - Slept in the shed again, ~2–3 hrs total sleep across both nights.
  - Drafted ground-rules letter (alcohol = I'm not in the space; detonation = I leave the room/house every time).

Tonight's data — the Laura discernment failure

Today I was depleted. Rested fine all day alone — no activation, no urge to produce. Laura got home just past 5pm. Chill for the first hour. Around 8pm started rebuilding the apartment finder. Told myself it was curiosity,but didn’t end up sleeping until 2am.

The pattern: activation correlated with her presence, not my energy. Body knew at 6-7pm. I overrode it.

The stuck line: I cannot reliably tell the difference between authentic solitude and defensive solitude in real-time. The story-brain rationalises both the same way.

The receiving gap (Lesson 07, tonight)

My curriculum has hundreds of reps for giving, fixing, processing, analysing. Almost zero for receiving. AI can mirror; it cannot co-regulate. The rep that actually moves the wound is with a human nervous system — and that's the rep I've barely logged.

The Kev piece

Bigger thing I'm sitting with: Kev gave me a gift of vulnerability weeks ago that landed deep. I have received it — but I haven't showed up how I want to by sharing me vulnerably: unpackaged. I avoided this in the texts because I thought I didn't &quotneed&quot the support — which is bullshit — I did AND I wanted to share that I've been struggling — to share it, not for a solution. Share it is an open thing, not a closed past circumstance.

Three questions

1. The discernment question. How do I build the in-body signal for authentic vs defensive solitude in real-time? The story-brain rationalises both the same way. Tonight is the data.

2. The receiving-rep question. What does a receiving rep with Laura look like that isn't me performing presence? What's the receiving rep that scares me most that I'm not naming?

Sidebar if time: Moving to Bangkok June 17th. New environment, just me and Laura, body still gripping. What needs sorting here vs there?

STRONG BLUEPRINT-How to Handle It

Whatever the really strong blueprint thing says to do, do the opposite. 

Do it to the strongest degree you feel capable of at the time. Doesn’t need to be deep, just being around other people.
Shoot Kev a txt, call Julz, go play pool be around other people

Across your week hit all 4 of these-The 4 Aces

Achievement-doing man shit to feel pride in yourself-masculine man shit
Connection-with another HUMAN 1st, animals, plants. With your boys, go to the dog park, being around nature. 
Enjoyment-things that genuinely give me joy-reading, TV, playing pool, socializing with friends. 
Self care-gym/self development, therapy session, getting blood work/planning out supplements etc

Here’s the psychological premise:

THE 4 ACES — Reg's Framework

Hit all 4 across the week. Miss one and things start tilting. Miss two and you're withdrawn, heavy, isolating.

1. ACHIEVEMENT
Doing man shit to feel pride in yourself. Masculine man shit.

2. CONNECTION
With another HUMAN first. Then animals, plants, nature.
Boys, Laura, dog park, being outside.

3. ENJOYMENT
Things that genuinely give you joy.
Reading, TV, pool, socialising with mates.

4. SELF CARE
Gym, self development, therapy, bloods, supplements, planning.

—

Rule: structure the calendar so all 4 get touched weekly. Don't leave to chance — especially when your usual crew for one ace is out of rotation.

When Connection drops, Enjoyment usually follows (shared activities die with it). That's the spiral. Catch it early.

Pat Reflection:

Bro this is a really clean piece of self-awareness. Let me reflect it back:
What you just said:
Laura’s dry month is net good. But you’ve used it as cover. Friday = drinking + dancing + frisky wasn’t just habit, it was your primary Connection + Enjoyment ritual with her. Take the alcohol out without consciously replacing the ritual, and the connection container goes with it. You didn’t notice until you were already a softened, dotted, less-than version of yourself.
The real insight:
“Life is so much better shared” is your operating principle. Solo work raised the floor — you can now be alone without collapsing. Good. But the ceiling, the actual juice of your life, still comes from shared. That hasn’t changed and shouldn’t change. You’re not built to thrive in extended solitude. You’re a Leo who needs his people and his woman and his moments of being seen and adored. That’s not a wound, that’s wiring.
Where the trap is:
You confused “I can be alone” with “I should be alone more.” Independence training became withdrawal practice. The dry month removed the ritual scaffolding without you noticing, and you defaulted to introspection because that’s the muscle you’ve been building. Meanwhile the actual aces — Connection + Enjoyment with Laura specifically — have been quietly starving.
The move:
You don’t need alcohol for frisky Fridays. You need the ritual — the energy, the music, the dancing, the being silly together, the not-being-in-your-head. Booze was the on-ramp, not the thing itself.

THERAPY SESSION INSIGHTS — MAY 2026

THE APRIL SHITSTORM — DIAGNOSIS

Not one problem. 4 amplifiers stacking on the same week:
- Boys away = co-regulation option gone (Tank esp — pool 6hrs/wk → 1hr)
- Blueprint episodes carried solo
- Sober Fridays without replacement ritual
- Solo Laura nights = already my most blueprint-sensitive zone

Structural, not a character flaw. Of course I wobbled.

—

WHAT WAS REALLY HAPPENING

Confused &quotI can be alone&quot with &quotI should be alone more.&quot
Independence training became withdrawal practice.
Dry month removed ritual scaffolding without me noticing.
Defaulted to introspection because that's the muscle I've been building.
Meanwhile Connection + Enjoyment with Laura quietly starving.

Used the dry month as a subconscious excuse to withdraw.
Withdrew from Laura specifically, on the nights she means the most, when I feel most vulnerable around her.
That's a pattern, not a moment.

—

THE OPERATING PRINCIPLE

&quotLife is so much better shared.&quot
Solo work raised the floor — I can be alone without collapsing. Good.
But the ceiling, the actual juice of my life, still comes from shared.
Not built to thrive in extended solitude.
Leo who needs his people AND his woman AND his moments of being seen and adored.
Not a wound. Wiring.

—

THE TRUE NORTH COST

Wired for kindness, openness, authenticity. Zero capacity for disingenuous malice. Withholding from Laura = legit painful.

The cost: nowhere to put withdrawal energy except inward.
Can't be passive aggressive, can't punish, can't manipulate, can't fake it.
So when something's off, it all goes into me.
The fatigue, the heaviness, the isolating = the price of true north.

The gift: Laura never has to decode me. What you see is what you get.

—

PERFORMANCE vs ALIGNMENT

Performance = doing it for the reaction. Will they love me, stay, think I'm strong.
Alignment = doing it because not doing it is a quiet betrayal of who I am.
Response becomes a bonus, not the point.

External validation is rented. Have to keep earning it.
Alignment is owned.

—

THE CURATED VERSION vs THE REAL ME

The fear:
Share the ugly, unpolished, blueprint-prone, scared, withdrawing version → seen as broken, disgusting, untouchable. They'll run.

The truth underneath:
Wound talking. Learned somewhere the ugly parts are unlovable.
Spent a lifetime only showing parts I've sorted, polished, redeemed into a lesson.

What I've been doing:
Collecting love for a character I play really well. The strong silent supportive one. The one with answers. The rock.

What I actually want:
Love for the man underneath. The messy, scared, true-north-tortured-by-his-own-wiring me.

The catch:
Can't get that praise without showing that version first.
Praise for the polished stuff doesn't land because I know it's for the curated version.

Kev called me out for not letting him in. He sees it. He wants in. I won't let him.

The outcomes:
- Aligned people lean in
- Misaligned people leave
- Both are wins
- The only loss = staying curated and being mildly loved by everyone while feeling alone inside

The principle:
Internal good = external sorts itself. As a by-product, not an intention.

—

THE MOVE

Share the open loop. Not the resolved version with a bow on it.
The &quotI don't know what to do with this yet&quot version.
Not performance vulnerability. Honesty.

Next blueprint episode, next wobble, next heavy day — text one of the boys BEFORE I've processed it. Not after. Not with a tidy summary.
&quotHey man, having a heavy one, can we talk?&quot
That's the rep that builds the new pattern.

Kev first.

—

NOT THERAPY WORK ANYMORE. IDENTITY WORK.

Fought so hard to figure out who I am and got nowhere.
Now I just want to be me authentically — always known internally, finally listening. 🤌🏻🙏💜

milestones.story:
Meg Session #9 Prep/Reflection— 26 May 2026

Introspection

Some good, none-no good. Too much-also not so good.
During the month of April swung way too far inward and it was for me experientially way way too much of a good thing. I trust myself now and I've practiced the reflecting and emotional processing muscle more than enough to know that I can lean into that whenever it's needed and whenever my instincts tell me to. But this is not a replacement for human connection and indirect co-regulating healing which I've experienced in the first couple of weeks of May as being incredibly wildly more therapeutic basis. 

I am a much balanced version of me for myself, first and foremost, when I'm regularly around my boys and different relationship balances and groups and settings and so on. And in those times, those experiences and moments with family and friends I find very fulfilling, very balancing, and they make me want to be more disciplined and organized in the solitude portion of life as well. 

Achievement

Building the skill=sense of achievement. 
Usng FULLY Ai-too much for me.
Using it as a tool, good place for me.
Again in April with the perfect shitstorm of all my boys being away and a relationship episode between Laura and I plus a whole bunch of activity escalation in the property world I swung really hard into the tasks and a bottomless pit of lo-fi box sticking that didn't really amount to a whole lot of anything I valued.
Since the 12th of May when I had my last session with Meg, I've a hell of a lot more code, but also really prioritised being far more direct work-wise and doing significantly more myself and using my AI system as a tool instead of doing through the AI Thailand has been so much more enjoyable, fulfilling, non-lingering in terms of a open type of lo-fi persistent anxiety sort of feel, which it was definitely when I was doing it the other way around. But also, without doubt, so much fucking more impactful and so much more progress actually being made in a meaningful way to me. 
Having great systems and building tools and a work to support this investment business is really fucking cool. But it's not a replacement for like doing the work because I like the process. I like doing shit and figuring stuff out and facing challenges and overcoming them. And that in and of itself, regardless of the outcome, is quite enjoyable and fulfilling and something that at this stage I have no desire to not to do either. 
just under zero percent fucks to give or desire to go back to any kind of physical business framework like Spartans was or to work 40 50 80 hours a week but I do like challenging myself I like making progress and figuring things out and I want to build this investment business in something that problem solving and exploring and bashing my way through shit produces 20k net months as well. 
Yes, I want the outcome as well. I deserve it. The only thing standing in the way of that is me believing in me and following that up with actions. But I want the actions too. If I could just push a button and be at those 20k months, I'm very certain it would hold about as much meaning as many of the other meaningless achievements that I've done up over the years. When I get their steam in my own kind of way, I'm looking forward to experiencing what that feels like after authentically pursuing a goal, sticking at it, showing up, facing the challenges, overcoming them, stacking up the learnings and building skill and mastery in this field. 

FRIENDSHIPS
Friendships are either roses or cacti

Roses need nurtring, inpit, support, love etc-if one person pulls back, it doesnt work quickly

Examples for me: Laura, Alex, Geoffro, Mum, Kevo to a degree. 

Cacti-few drops here and there, but the relationship picks up where it leaves off more or less

People with ADHD tend to have way more dicentric relationships, which is very much correlated with their neurodivergent brains and significantly depleted ability for time awareness, especially in the longer term.  
I resonate with this very much because my depth perception historically in months and years is nothing near remotely accurate but I also feel, see and understand people and relationships and dynamics really really intensely and quickly as well with people I have known for a long time, but even with people I align with that I've just met or objectively don't really know particularly well at all like Caleb for example. 

There isn't a right or wrong when it comes to the balance of relationships and looking friendships and relationships in my life and the one the ones that I value the most and tend to have the biggest quantity of as well there is a strong representation of cacti type relationships and I think I'm pretty good with that. 
I'm very authentic, strongly instinct and value led and one of the fundamental tenets of strong lasting relationships is alignment, which is far more about who you are authentically more so than what particular word or act you do in a particular moment. 
That being said, especially now more than ever, as we prepare for this move to Bangkok am very mindful that all relationships still take effort, thought, consideration, action and input and even if it is a more cacti type relationship, that's not a excuse for avoiding or pouring into that relationship in whatever way that feels right for me internally. 

Calling, texting, sending a voice note, video calling, initiating, inviting, hosting, asking other people to go to things, asking to go to things myself, asking for us to go to things and vice versa. All of these are behaviours that I want to be showing far more than I would naturally do, especially in the first month of us landing in Bangkok and getting settled in. But more so than that, creating our environment of people, which is not only important to us, but is something that's really important to me, that I want to prioritise and I know that I need to face that fear and discomfort of meeting new people, starting conversations, introducing myself, asking questions about other people, etc. 
Deep down, I can't pretend I don't see how great of a friend, brother, husband, person that I am to be around and how much good that I bring to the room. Not just by being me, but also how much I bring out so much more of the best in other people, seemingly without even trying to, just as a consequence of being myself and my hopeful, abundant, optimistic, enthusiastic, let's fucking go kind of energy and approach to life. I jar with people I jar with and the ones that I don't, I'm good with that. For me it's really important that I make those determinations looking back, not looking forward, especially in those times when I might have tended to withdraw or avoid or rely on Laura because life is so much better shared and I do like meeting new people and being curious and getting excited and having a sprinkle of new and novel in my life as well. And that's something that ultimately like I am responsible for. 

This is an area where I very much look up to Laura and I'm so amazed and brazen and reckless and courageous she is when it comes to going up to strangers and talking to them and asking questions and so on. And I'm really proud of her for that. It's very inspiring to me. However, in that for me, because as much as I know that she would, if I asked, and that she will, to a degree for us and meeting new groups and couples and doing a lot of things. But the meat part of that equation is something that I need to do for me. And it's a little scary and a little daunting, and there's definitely some identity flashbacks of how bottled up and isolated that I was previously living in Bangkok, which was very much due to my own behaviour, my own choices, and 100% a perceptive barrier, not a real one. 
I didn't meet new people. I didn't engage in conversations. I avoided, withdraw, stuck to what I knew and lo-fi hoped that someone I kind of knew would introduce me to someone else and so on and Spartans it was a really quite lonely existence and I was very dependent on my ex which I didn't enjoy that over-indexing in that area at all. I don't think that was not only net good for the relationship, but above all else it was not net good for me because I was being a lesser version of myself that I wasn't proud of internally. 

So fuck that noise. We're going to hit the ground running. Going to explore a bunch of gyms. Join one or two. Ideally, actually, I'm going to commit to joining two gyms, or at least frequenting two gyms at a minimum. And a pool hall and make an effort to go new and different places on a regular basis to grab some food after training or whatever, just something of any type.  I'm not as worried about the staying connected with my fucking amazing family by choice here in Australia. I trust myself in that area. I've got the framework and the reminders and all that kind of thing. But the in-person part is important too. And as much as I've got such an amazing network of a big, beautiful family I could have never dreamt of even a few years ago that I have so abundantly today, it's really important to me that I also push myself and face those fears and challenges to meet new people, open new doors, expand my horizons because I like that version of me far more. 
It's not about meeting anyone in particular. So much of the greatest experiences and people that are in my life now have come as a result of the networks that I've grown and expanded over time previously. And I really believe that the best people and the greatest relationships I've ever experienced are still very much ahead of me.  both in the expansion of some existing relationships in ways I have absolutely no idea will show up, but also in people I'm yet to meet at all. And it's a little bit daunting and scary, but it's also really fucking exciting as well and gets my butt tingling and a real fizzy curiosity to explore life and dream and be open and expansive. And although it's not the intention, I can very much see that that happens to lead to a far bigger, better network, bigger, better in so many other areas as well, including financially, which is important to me. 

ADHD Time Blindness

Time blindness is a neurological quirk where the brain struggles to perceive, measure, or track the passing of time. It is not laziness, bad manners, or a lack of caring; it is a literal sensory deficit. [1, 2, 3, 4]
The process of getting diagnosed requires massive administrative effort, which directly targets the weakest parts of an ADHD brain. [1]

🧠 The Core Concept: Two Time Zones
The ADHD brain generally recognizes only two time horizons: [1]

NOW: Whatever is currently happening, interesting, or right in front of you.
NOT NOW: Everything else, whether it is due in 5 minutes, 5 days, or 5 months.

🚦 Why it Happens (The Science-Y Bit)

Faulty Internal Clock: Neurotypical brains pass time with an automatic, unconscious radar. The ADHD prefrontal cortex struggles to do this.
Dopamine Deficiency: The brain requires dopamine to register interest and urgency. If a task lacks excitement, the brain cannot perceive the urgency of time closing in.
Hyperfocus Trap: When a task triggers high dopamine, the brain enters &quotautomatic attention&quot. It locks in so deeply that hours feel like minutes. [, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]

🌪️ How it Manifests Every Day

Time Underestimation: Believing a 30-minute drive plus a shower will somehow only take 10 minutes total.
Time Overestimation: Avoiding a 5-minute task because the brain scales it up to feel like a gruelling, 4-hour ordeal.
The &quotWaiting Mode&quot Paralysis: Inability to do anything at 10:00 AM because of an appointment at 2:00 PM, out of fear that &quotNow&quot will swallow up the time.
Temporal Discounting: Consequences feel completely unreal until they are directly in the &quotNow&quot zone.

🛠️ Working With It
The goal is to stop relying on an internal clock and make time visible externally.

Visual Timers: Devices like the Time Timer use a disappearing red disc to show time physically shrinking.
Alarms for Transitions: Setting alarms for when to start preparing to leave, rather than just when to leave.
External Clocks: Placing analogue clocks in every room so time remains a visible, physical presence.

🗺️ The &quotTime-Stretching&quot Memory Phenomenon
The reason your detailed memory fails at dating events is that ADHD brains store memories based on emotional intensity, not a chronological timeline. A vivid event from seven years ago feels just as fresh as something from last year because the detail is still so crisp. It is a sign of a rich memory, not a broken mind.

The Science of the Relationship Types

🌹 Rose Friendships: High-Maintenance &amp Deep
These relationships are beautiful, fragrant, and deeply rewarding, but they are highly sensitive to environmental changes. [1]

Investment: They require consistent communication, vulnerability, and shared time.
The Risk: If one person stops watering the relationship, it can wither and die quickly.
The Reward: Exceptional emotional intimacy and a strong sense of mutual security. [1, 2, 3, 4]

🌵 Cactus Friendships: Low-Maintenance &amp Resilient
These bonds are incredibly durable and built to survive long periods of drought, distance, or busy life chapters. [1]

Investment: Minimal day-to-day contact; a quick text or a yearly meetup is enough.
The Risk: They can sometimes feel distant if you go through a crisis and need immediate, intense support.
The Reward: Effortless connection that completely bypasses awkwardness, picking up exactly where you left off. [1]

🌿 The Third Type: Evergreen Friendships
Many people also experience &quotEvergreen&quot friendships, which sit comfortably right between the two.

The Balance: They do not need daily watering like a rose, but they cannot survive on absolute neglect like a cactus.
The Routine: They thrive on a predictable, steady rhythm—like a monthly dinner or a weekly phone call.

Recognising which category your friends fall into prevents burnout and helps you manage your expectations.
